methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $26M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Helios is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Jonathan Geifman and Dor Blum that has raised $26M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 9 private companies and 1 public company in the climate and energy sector, including peers such as Graphyte, Fourth Power, Steady Energy, TAE Technologies, and Redwood Materials.

Two triangulation methods are applicable. The stage-progression method applies a 2.0x multiplier to the $26M raised proxy, implying a $52M valuation. The peer-median method examines three same-sector, same-stage comparables—Graphyte at $200M, Fourth Power at $150M, and Steady Energy at $110M—yielding a median valuation of $150M with a range of $110M to $200M. These companies serve as direct benchmarks for recent Series A funding dynamics in the climate tech space.

Triangulating across both methods produces a valuation range of $44M (low) to $172.5M (high), with a median implied valuation of $101M. The spread reflects meaningful uncertainty around the appropriate valuation multiple for a Series A climate tech company. Stage-progression anchors conservatively near the capital-raised floor; peer-median reflects recent market pricing but is sensitive to the composition of the three-company sample. Key uncertainties include the degree to which Helios's technology differentiation and market position align with the selected peer set, and whether post-Series A capital conditions will sustain current market multiples.
